natural_occurrence.description: Stannous chloride does not occur naturally. It is synthetically manufactured for use in food processing and industrial applications.

manufacturing.description: Produced by dissolving metallic tin in hydrochloric acid. The resulting tin(II) chloride is purified and crystallized. Must be kept in acidic conditions to prevent oxidation to tin(IV) chloride.

other_uses.industrial: Electroplating, reducing agent in chemical synthesis, catalyst, glass production.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Reducing agent in pharmaceutical synthesis, stabilizer for hydrogen peroxide.

eu.conditions: Limited use in specific canned and bottled foods for color retention. Not permitted in all categories.

usa.notes: Limited to specific uses in canned foods, maximum 15 ppm in asparagus.

japan.notes: Limited use with maximum residue limits.

australia_nz.notes: Restricted use with maximum permitted levels.

canada.notes: Limited to specific applications in canned foods.

regulatory_history.event: EFSA re-evaluated stannous chloride and established a lower ADI than JECFA due to concerns about tin exposure from multiple sources including canned foods.

commonly_found_in: Canned soft drinks in tin cans (rare now)
fun_facts: Used specifically to keep canned WHITE ASPARAGUS looking white and not turning gray/brown
Acts as a reducing agent, preventing oxidation reactions that cause color changes in canned foods
Less commonly used today due to concerns about tin exposure and the shift to aluminum cans
Tin cans (actually steel with tin coating) can leach small amounts of tin into food, which is why there are strict limits
One of the few food additives containing a metal (tin) that isn't essential to human nutrition
The ADI is set for total tin exposure, not just from stannous chloride - regulators consider tin from can coatings too
